title: Peruvian Lomo Saltado

ingredients.name: beef sirloin or rump, sliced into thin strips

subs.name: chicken strips or paneer for non-beef households — marinate and cook the same way

ingredients.name: potatoes, cut into thick matchstick strips

subs.name: cassava chips or sweet potato fries for an authentic Peruvian-style variation

ingredients.name: capsicum (red or yellow), cut into strips
spring onions, cut into 4 cm lengths

subs.name: 2 tbsp soy sauce can be replaced with 1 tbsp tamarind concentrate and 1 tbsp salt-water for a soy-free version

steps.text: Marinate the beef strips with 1 tbsp soy sauce, half the cumin and a pinch of salt for 10 minutes. Meanwhile, deep-fry or shallow-fry the potato strips in hot oil until golden and crispy — about 5–6 minutes. Drain and set aside; season with salt.

Heat a wok or kadai over the highest possible flame until smoking. Add 2 tbsp oil and, when it shimmers, add the beef strips in a single layer without stirring for 1 minute to get a sear. Toss and cook for another 2 minutes — the beef should be browned outside but still slightly pink inside. Remove and set aside.

In the same wok, add 1 tbsp oil on high flame. Add the minced garlic and stir for 20 seconds, then add the capsicum and spring onion. Stir-fry for 2 minutes until they start to soften at the edges but retain crunch.

Add the tomato wedges and toss for 1 minute — they should stay chunky, not dissolve. Return the beef to the wok. Pour in the remaining soy sauce, vinegar, remaining cumin and the sliced green chilli. Toss everything together on high heat for 1–2 minutes until the sauce reduces and coats the ingredients.

Scatter coriander leaves over the stir-fry and toss once more. Serve immediately over white rice with the crispy potato strips arranged alongside or piled on top. The contrast between the saucy stir-fry and the crunchy potatoes is the defining character of the dish.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: beef → chicken strips or paneer for non-beef households — marinate and cook the same way; soy-sauce → 2 tbsp soy sauce can be replaced with 1 tbsp tamarind concentrate and 1 tbsp salt-water for a soy-free version; potato → cassava chips or sweet potato fries for an authentic Peruvian-style variation
indian_kitchen_adaptation: The wok technique is identical to Indo-Chinese stir-frying already popular in India. Use your heaviest kadai on maximum flame, add the oil and let it smoke before adding meat — high heat is non-negotiable. Light soy sauce is widely available at Indian supermarkets and online. Beef can be substituted with chicken for a crowd that doesn't eat red meat. The fried potato strips are very similar to Indian French fries — use your standard kadai for deep-frying.
